title: AI News Summarizer
description: AI powered news analysis app that fetches articles from APIs, generates summaries using BART large CNN, classifies topics with BART MNLI, and performs sentiment analysis with DistilBERT through a React frontend and PyTorch backend.
content:
**GitHub:** [https://github.com/devwo1f/ai-news-summariser](https://github.com/devwo1f/ai-news-summariser)


## AI News Summarizer and Sentiment Analyzer


This project is an end to end AI powered news analysis system that automatically collects news articles from external APIs, generates concise summaries, classifies article topics, and performs sentiment analysis to determine whether the news is positive, neutral, or negative. The backend pipeline processes raw news text using transformer based NLP models to extract meaningful insights from large volumes of articles.


For summarization, the system uses the **BART large CNN transformer model**, which generates abstractive summaries that capture the key information of each article. Sentiment analysis is performed using **DistilBERT**, enabling efficient classification of news sentiment with low inference latency. Additionally, **BART large MNLI** is used for zero shot topic classification, allowing articles to be categorized dynamically without task specific training.


The backend is implemented in **Python with PyTorch**, handling model inference and news data processing, while the frontend is built with **React and JavaScript** to provide an interactive interface where users can browse summarized news articles along with their sentiment and topic labels.


### Tech Stack


Python, PyTorch, React, JavaScript, Transformer based NLP models (BART large CNN, BART large MNLI, DistilBERT)

title: BraTS Brain Tumor Segmentation and Clinical Metrology Pipeline
description: A modular, OOP-based PyTorch pipeline for automated brain tumor segmentation from MRI scans using the BraTS 2020 dataset. The system trains a U-Net deep learning model on 4-channel MRI inputs, segments three hierarchical tumor sub-regions, and extracts RANO clinical metrology measurements for quantitative tumor assessment.
content:
**Github:** [https://github.com/devwo1f/brats-segmentation](https://github.com/devwo1f/brats-segmentation)


## BraTS 2020 Brain Tumor Segmentation and Clinical Metrology Pipeline


This project implements an end to end deep learning pipeline for **automated brain tumor segmentation and quantitative tumor measurement from multi modal MRI scans**. The system trains a **U Net based semantic segmentation model in PyTorch** to identify tumor regions in MRI slices and computes clinically relevant measurements based on the **RANO (Response Assessment in Neuro Oncology) criteria** used in neuro oncology research and treatment evaluation.


The model is trained on the **BraTS 2020 dataset**, which contains multi modal MRI scans from hundreds of patients with gliomas. Each MRI study includes four imaging modalities, **T1, T1 contrast enhanced, T2, and FLAIR**, which are combined as a **4 channel input tensor**. The network performs pixel level segmentation of three hierarchical tumor regions:


Whole Tumor (WT)


Tumor Core (TC)


Enhancing Tumor (ET)


These regions follow a nested hierarchy where ET is contained within TC and TC is contained within WT. The model outputs three segmentation masks corresponding to these tumor sub regions for each MRI slice.


The core segmentation architecture is a **31 million parameter U Net encoder decoder network with skip connections**. The encoder progressively extracts spatial features while reducing resolution through convolutional blocks and pooling layers, while the decoder upsamples feature maps and fuses them with high resolution skip connections to recover precise spatial boundaries. The model produces **3 channel segmentation logits** that are converted to probabilities using sigmoid activation during inference.


Training is implemented in **PyTorch with mixed precision (FP16) training using torch autocast and GradScaler**, enabling faster training and reduced memory consumption on an **NVIDIA RTX 4060 GPU with 8 GB VRAM**. The optimization objective combines **Binary Cross Entropy with Dice Loss**, balancing pixel level classification accuracy with region level overlap performance, which is particularly important for medical segmentation tasks where tumor regions may occupy only a small fraction of the image.


To ensure reliable evaluation, the dataset is split at the **patient volume level rather than slice level**, preventing data leakage where adjacent slices from the same patient appear in both training and validation sets. Data augmentation using **Albumentations** introduces spatial transformations such as flips, rotations, brightness variations, and noise to improve model generalization.


Beyond segmentation, the system extracts **quantitative tumor measurements used in clinical oncology workflows**. Using **OpenCV based contour extraction**, the segmented tumor regions are converted into geometric contours from which the pipeline calculates:


Major and minor perpendicular tumor diameters


Cross sectional tumor area


Bidimensional product of diameters (key RANO metric)


These measurements are aggregated into structured reports and exported as **CSV and JSON files**, enabling downstream clinical analysis, treatment monitoring, and integration with research pipelines.


The project follows a **modular object oriented architecture**, separating responsibilities across data loading, model definition, training logic, evaluation metrics, and clinical metrology. Configuration management is handled through YAML based configuration files with strongly typed dataclass wrappers, allowing experiments to be reproduced and tuned easily.


The pipeline also includes **comprehensive unit testing using pytest**, verifying dataset loading, model outputs, loss functions, and RANO measurement calculations using synthetic data. This ensures reliability of the entire segmentation and measurement pipeline without requiring access to the full dataset during testing.


### Tech Stack


Machine Learning


PyTorch, U Net, Albumentations, NumPy, Scikit learn


Medical Image Processing


OpenCV, HDF5, MRI multi modal imaging


Training Optimization


Mixed precision training (FP16), AdamW optimizer, early stopping


Data Engineering


HDF5 datasets, YAML configuration management, dataclass based configs


Evaluation and Testing


Dice coefficient, IoU metrics, PyTest based testing framework


### Key Features


Multi modal MRI segmentation using a U Net architecture


Pixel level segmentation of three hierarchical tumor regions


Mixed precision training optimized for consumer GPUs


Automated extraction of RANO clinical tumor measurements


Configurable training pipeline with YAML based experiment configs


Unit tested modular architecture for reliable experimentation

title: Smart Traffic Sign Recognition
slug: A production-grade traffic sign recognition system
description: A production-grade traffic sign recognition system using YOLOv8 (detection) + EfficientNet (classification) with real-time video processing, powered by PyTorch, FastAPI, and a modern TypeScript dashboard.
content:
**GitHub Link**:  [https://github.com/devwo1f/smart-traffic-sign-recognition](https://github.com/devwo1f/smart-traffic-sign-recognition)


## Smart Traffic Sign Recognition and Review System


This project implements a **production oriented traffic sign recognition pipeline** that combines object detection, fine grained classification, real time inference, and a full stack monitoring dashboard. The system detects traffic signs from images or video streams, classifies them into hundreds of categories, and logs predictions for analysis through an API driven backend and a TypeScript dashboard.


The core architecture follows a **two stage deep learning pipeline**. First, a **YOLOv8x object detection model** identifies candidate traffic sign regions within an image or video frame. Detected bounding boxes are then cropped and passed into an **EfficientNet B3 classifier**, which performs fine grained classification of the traffic sign category. This separation of detection and classification improves accuracy compared to whole image classification and enables the system to operate effectively in real world scenes where multiple objects appear simultaneously.


The models are trained using the **Mapillary Traffic Sign Dataset (MTSD)**, which contains more than **200k annotated images and 300 plus traffic sign classes** captured under diverse environmental conditions. A preprocessing pipeline extracts sign crops, generates training splits, and prepares datasets in YOLO format for detection training.


The machine learning pipeline is implemented in **PyTorch**, with training, evaluation, benchmarking, and model export handled through modular scripts. The EfficientNet B3 classifier is trained with mixed precision training, gradient accumulation, and optimized data loading strategies to efficiently utilize an **NVIDIA RTX 4060 GPU with 8 GB VRAM**. These optimizations include parallel dataloaders, prefetching, persistent workers, and cuDNN kernel autotuning, which improved training throughput from roughly **5.3 to 10.4 iterations per second**.


For inference and deployment, trained models are exported to **ONNX format** to enable hardware accelerated inference across platforms. Optional **TensorRT quantization** is used to further optimize performance, achieving approximately **3× faster inference compared to native PyTorch** and enabling real time processing at roughly **60 FPS**.


The backend service is implemented using **FastAPI**, providing REST endpoints for image, batch, and video predictions as well as a WebSocket interface for real time webcam streaming. Predictions and inference metadata are stored in **PostgreSQL**, allowing historical inspection logs to be queried and filtered through the API. The backend also exposes a retraining endpoint to trigger model updates and supports semantic versioning of trained models.


On the frontend, a lightweight **TypeScript and HTML dashboard** visualizes model outputs and inspection logs. The dashboard supports video playback with bounding box overlays rendered using an HTML5 canvas synchronized with a requestAnimationFrame loop, allowing traffic sign detections to track accurately over video frames in real time.


The entire system can be deployed locally or in containerized environments using **Docker Compose**, enabling reproducible full stack deployment including the ML pipeline, API server, frontend dashboard, and PostgreSQL database.
